title: Scorecards: Building Structure and Expectations for Your Team
summary: Erin Stetzer walked participants through her company's scorecard system for project managers, explaining how scorecards provide structure around five core business pillars: time, money, data, people, and results. The session included a live walkthrough of the project manager scorecard template and covered non-negotiables around scheduling, communication, and budget management. An extended Q&A addressed hiring criteria, transitioning tradespeople into management roles, PM fees, ordering responsibilities, and the company's lean organizational structure.

teachings.explanation: Scorecards are not tools to control people but to clearly define expectations so that everyone knows the rules in advance. Like athletic free throw standards, when people know what is expected, they rise to meet it consistently.
teachings.quote: Scorecards, to me, it's not meant to micromanage people. What it's meant to do is give structure to expectations.

teachings.concept: The Five Pillars of Every Business Decision
teachings.explanation: Every plan, decision, and role in a company can be evaluated through five lenses: time, money, data (information), people, and results. Using these as a meeting agenda or scorecard framework ensures nothing important is overlooked.
teachings.quote: Every single decision that we make, every plan that we make, every thought we have is dialed into those five things.

teachings.concept: The Four-Week Accurate Schedule with Trade Commitments
teachings.explanation: A four-week schedule is the optimal window for construction planning. Two weeks is too short to react; six or eight weeks shifts too much. Critically, the schedule must reflect actual commitments from trades, not be fabricated to satisfy the owner.
teachings.quote: I produce an accurate four week schedule with commitments from trades. This is not a poof, I'm going to put a bunch of words down and pretend I've talked to everybody.

teachings.concept: Project Managers Must Know the Plans Better Than Anyone
teachings.explanation: A project manager's core competency is deeply studying construction documents so they can answer questions from architects, designers, and clients authoritatively, catch errors in advance, and bring solutions before problems arise.
teachings.quote: If it's your project, you know it better than the architect. You know it better than the designer. You definitely know it better than I do. And you know it better than the client.

teachings.explanation: Every action in a project should produce a result. When something stalls mid-process, it signals a bottleneck. A project manager's job is not to leave loops open or pretend they are closed, but to actively solve the problem and bring things full circle.
teachings.quote: A project manager's job is to solve it, not just pretend the loop is closed and move on.

teachings.concept: Job Site Videos as a Management and Engagement Tool
teachings.explanation: Having project managers record and share daily job site videos serves multiple purposes: it slows them down to think, keeps the owner informed, reveals the PM's mindset and energy level, and surprisingly increases PMs' sense of ownership over their projects.
teachings.quote: They're more engaged in their projects because of the videos. Like it's the weirdest thing. And I don't know what the science is behind it, but it has been a game changer for us.

teachings.explanation: Tradespeople and hands-on builders transitioning into project management roles struggle to shift from executing work themselves to directing others. This is a mindset change that takes time, structure, and coaching, and not everyone will make the transition successfully.
teachings.quote: There's a happiness factor in doing things, too. Some people get it and some people don't. They don't want to.

teachings.concept: Lean Organizational Structure with Separated Lanes
teachings.explanation: Keeping project management and financial management in clearly separated roles, connected by one bridge person who manages modifications and communication, creates accountability without confusion. Staying lean avoids the trap of bloated payroll forcing owners to take unwanted projects.
teachings.quote: We do keep money, project management, and money separate... We've intentionally stayed around 10 people, because when we start getting into the 12, 15, 20, we're now going to silo these teams.

actionable_advice: Put the daily schedule update time into the project manager's calendar as a non-negotiable recurring appointment, just as owners block meetings.
Require project managers to produce an accurate four-week schedule with confirmed trade commitments, not a placeholder schedule meant to satisfy the owner.
Have project managers produce a broader three-month projected schedule on the first of each month to maintain a wide lens while also executing in the near term.
Use time, money, people, data, and results as the standing agenda for weekly PM meetings so managers come prepared to drive the conversation rather than being pulled for information.
Have project managers record and upload daily job site videos to a shared folder; review them each morning (e.g., between 6:00–6:30 a.m.) and provide feedback or recognition.
When onboarding a new project manager, check in with them daily for the first four months and give up to three reminders before escalating non-compliance to a direct conversation about fit.
Write down the specific hiring criteria your business needs right now based on your current season and size, rather than copying a generic job description.
Place the project manager fee below the line of cost of work in proposals to avoid charging overhead fees on top of it, which clients resist.
Review cash flow on a weekly basis across all business lines to know when you are positioned to hire and to understand your negotiating position on any proposal.
Have project managers approve or flag invoices in a shared folder before each weekly team meeting so the financial and project management lanes stay separate but synchronized.
When meeting with potential clients, clearly articulate your deliverables in the contract so the value of your management fee is self-evident and expected.
client_challenges: How to transition a long-time jack-of-all-trades employee from a hands-on trade mindset to a project management mindset, including getting him to coach rather than dismiss workers who don't meet his standards.
How to get project managers to come to weekly meetings truly prepared to drive the conversation about their projects, rather than requiring the owner to pull information from them.
How to determine hiring criteria for project managers at different stages of business growth, and whether to find job posting templates or formats that attract high-standard candidates.
How to justify and communicate a project management fee to potential clients who compare proposals from builders with no such fee.
How to determine the right number of projects per project manager without overloading them, and what a PM's day actually looks like when managing one or two projects.
How to decide which materials and ordering responsibilities belong to the PM versus the trade, especially for items like windows, framing, tile, and wood floors.
How to calculate the project manager fee and whether it should be a percentage of cost, a monthly fee, or tied to salary plus overhead, and how to separate it from the owner fee.
How to scale from under $2 million in revenue while keeping payroll lean and avoiding the trap of hiring too many layers of superintendents and assistants before cash flow supports it.
notable_quotes: On free throw standards as a model for business: 'You better believe people are practicing and you're going to be a 92% free throw shooter, period. But it's because people know the rules.'
On building systems: 'If you want to build an engine that works, copy this for real. This engine works. Now, getting people to run the engine, that's what we need to do, but this is the engine.'
On staying competitive: 'If you're staying the same, you're actually losing. You really are. You're losing time. You're losing effectiveness.'

On hiring based on scorecards: 'Just because they've been building for 10 years does not mean they fit this level of standards. People appreciate the clarity. Know exactly what you want and hire them.'
On communicating deliverables to clients: 'When you hire a building company, the clearer you are on the deliveries, the more often you're going to get hired.'
On lean business freedom: 'It feels really good to turn a job away that I just don't want to do. And I don't feel handcuffed to take every job that comes across our plate.'
